title: Why World History Matters
body:
World history matters because the present only makes sense when you know what produced it. Colonial borders, economic inequality, migration patterns, language spread, and political conflict all have deep historical roots. If you do not know the history, the present looks random when it is not.

The subject also matters because it builds humility. Every past civilisation believed it understood the world better than those before it. Studying history reminds us how often confident societies were wrong, and how quickly power can shift.

Finally, world history matters because it gives voice to people who were ignored by older narratives. It helps us see history not just as a sequence of rulers and wars, but as the story of ordinary people, labour, exchange, belief, and survival.
keyPoints: History explains the roots of modern politics and inequality
It reveals long-term patterns in power and collapse
It encourages humility about present assumptions
It widens history beyond elites and empires
It helps explain culture, borders, and identity
It turns the present into something legible
furtherReading: 'The Wretched of the Earth' by Frantz Fanon
